title: Interpret constants of proportionality
description: Interpret the meaning of constants of proportionality in tables, equations, contexts, and diagrams.
topic_path: Compare and interpret constants of proportionality

assessment_items.question_content:
Betty's Bakery calculates the total price $d$ in dollars for $c$ cupcakes using the equation $d=2c$.

**What does $2$ mean in this situation?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We can write a verbal equation to relate the total price and the number of cupcakes.

$\maroonD{\text{total price}} = \blueD{\text{price per cupcake}} \cdot \greenD{\text{number of cupcakes}}$

Let's fill in the variables we already know into our equation.

$\maroonD{d} = \blueD{[\text{price per cupcake}]}\cdot \greenD{c}$

Let's compare this equation with our original equation.

$\maroonD{d} = \blueD{2} \greenD{c}$
The bakery charges $\$2$ per cupcake.

choices.content: The bakery charges $\$2$ for each cupcake.
The bakery sells $2$ cupcakes for a dollar.
The bakery sells $2$ types of cupcakes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Casey types at a constant speed, as shown in the following table.

Words typed | Seconds
:-: | :-:
$1$ | $5$
$2$ | $10$
$7$ | $35$
$12$ | $60$

**What does $5$ mean in this situation?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: The table describes a proportional relationship between Casey's number of words typed and the number of seconds that have gone by.  Casey has typed $1$ word after $5$ seconds.

When another $5$ seconds have gone by (for a total of $10$ seconds), Casey has typed $2$ words.

The pattern continues so that every $5$ seconds, Casey finishes typing another word.
Casey takes $5$ seconds to type a word.

choices.content: Casey types $5$ words per second.
Casey types $5$ words per minute.
Casey takes $5$ seconds to type a word.

assessment_items.question_content:
Maya shaved her head and then began letting her hair grow. She represents the length $\ell$ of her hair, in centimeters, $m$ months after she shaved her head using the equation $\ell=1.25m$.

**What does $1.25$ mean in this situation?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We can write a verbal equation to relate the length of Maya's hair to the number of months that have passed.

$\maroonD{\text{length of hair}} = \blueD{\text{hair growth per month}} \cdot \greenD{\text{number of months}}$

Let's fill in the variables we already know into our equation.

$\maroonD{\ell} = \blueD{[\text{hair growth per month}]} \cdot \greenD{m}$

Let's compare this equation with our original equation.

$\maroonD{\ell} = \blueD{1.25} \greenD{m}$
Maya's hair grows $1.25$ centimeters per month.

choices.content: It has been $1.25$ months since Maya shaved her head.
Maya's hair grows $1.25$ centimeters per month.
Maya's hair takes $1.25$ months to grow a centimeter longer.

assessment_items.question_content:
Zhen is an audiobook narrator. The table gives some completed audio lengths and the amount of time Zhen spends researching, recording, and editing the audiobook.

Completed length of audiobook (hours) | Working time (hours)
:-:|:-:
$1$ | $6\dfrac{1}{2}$
$3\dfrac{1}{2}$| $22\dfrac{3}{4}$
$7$| $45\dfrac{1}{2}$
$10$|$65$

**What does $6\dfrac{1}{2}$ mean in this situation?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: Zhen spends different amounts of time working on different books, so $6\dfrac{1}{2}$ does *not* represent the amount of time she spends per book.
Zhen spends longer than an hour per book for each book listed in the table, so she does *not* complete $6\dfrac{1}{2}$ books per hour.

The table describes a proportional relationship between the length of the completed audiobook and the hours Zhen spends working on it.  

It takes Zhen $6\dfrac{1}{2}$ hours of working time to complete a $1$ hour long audiobook. Each additional hour of the length of the completed audiobook increases the working time by $6\dfrac{1}{2}$ hours.
Zhen spends $6\dfrac{1}{2}$ hours working for every hour of the completed audiobook.

choices.content: Zhen spends $6\dfrac{1}{2}$ hours working for every hour of the completed audiobook.
Zhen completes $6\dfrac{1}{2}$ audiobooks in an hour.
Zhen spends $6\dfrac{1}{2}$ hours working per audiobook.

assessment_items.question_content:
The receipt below shows the money, in dollars, spent by a customer at the Good Groceries store.

$\begin{array}{l r}
\huge{\text{Good Groceries}}\\
11/03/2019	&4:55\,\text{PM}\\
\hline
\begin{array}
{r l}
	&\text{Lettuce}\\
3\ \text{kg} &@ 1.20/\text{kg}
\end{array}	&3.60\\
\hline
\text{SUBTOTAL}	&3.60\\
\text{TOTAL TENDERED}	&4.00\\
\text{CHANGE}		&0.40
\end{array}$

**What does $1.20$ mean in this situation?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: The receipt tells us that $1.20$ is per kilogram of lettuce. We know that the cost is measured in dollars.

We can make sure that this interpretation makes sense by making a verbal equation and checking it with the information given.

$\maroonD{\text{total cost}} = \blueD{\text{cost per kilogram}} \cdot \greenD{\text{number of kilograms}}$

The customer bought $\greenD{3}$ kilograms of lettuce and paid $\maroonD{3.60}$ dollars for it.

$\maroonD{3.60} \stackrel{\checkmark}{=} \blueD{1.20} \cdot \greenD{3}$
Good Groceries charges $1.20$ dollars per kilogram of lettuce.

choices.content: The customer bought $1.20$ kilograms of lettuce.
The customer can buy $1.20$ kilograms of lettuce for a dollar.
Good Groceries charges $1.20$ dollars per kilogram of lettuce.

assessment_items.question_content:
Solomon is $3\text{D}$ printing chess pieces. The table gives the amount of time needed to print certain numbers of pieces. 

Pieces | Print time (minutes)
:-: | :-:
$1$ | $16$
$2$ | $32$
$8$ | $128$
$32$ | $512$

**What does $16$ mean in this situation?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: The question does not mention anything about chess sets, so even though each side has $16$ pieces in chess, $16$ does *not* represent the number of pieces in each chess set Solomon prints.
According to the table, printing fewer than $16$ pieces takes longer than $1$ minute, so $16$ does *not* represent the number of chess pieces Solomon can print in a minute.

The table describes a proportional relationship between the number of chess pieces printed and the amount of time required.

It takes Solomon $16$ minutes to print $1$ chess piece. Each additional chess piece takes an additional $16$ minutes to print.
It takes Solomon $16$ minutes to print each chess piece.

choices.content: It takes Solomon $16$ minutes to print each chess piece.
Each chess set Solomon prints contains $16$ pieces.
Solomon can print $16$ chess pieces in a minute.

assessment_items.question_content:
The partial receipt below shows the money, in dollars, Ryan spent at an online clothing retailer.

PRODUCT | QTY | PRICE | TOTAL
:- | :-: | :-: | :-:
Socks | $3$ | $2.00$ | $6.00$
V-Neck | $2$ | $8.00$ | $16.00$
Shorts | $1$ | $15.00$ | $15.00$


**What does $8.00$ mean in this situation?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: The number $8.00$ appears in the price column of the receipt. We know that price is measured in dollars.

We can make sure that this interpretation makes sense by making a verbal equation and checking it with the information given.

$\greenD{\text{QTY}}\cdot\blueD{\text{PRICE}}=\maroonD{\text{TOTAL}}$

Ryan bought $\greenD{2}$ V-Necks for a total price of $\maroonD{16}$ dollars.

$\greenD{2}\cdot\blueD{8.00} \stackrel{\checkmark}{=} \maroonD{16.00}$
The price of each V-Neck Ryan bought was $8.00$ dollars.

choices.content: Ryan bought $8.00$ V-Necks.
The price of each V-Neck Ryan bought was $8.00$ dollars.
The total price of the V-Necks Ryan bought was $8.00$ dollars.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jackson bought $5$ ounces of raisins for $\$4$.  

**How much do raisins cost per ounce?**

$\$$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]


**How many ounces of raisins can be bought for $\$1$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] ounces
hints:
We can write a verbal equation to relate the price and the amount of raisins.

$\maroonD{\text{total price}} = \greenD{\text{number of ounces}} \cdot \blueD{\text{price per ounce}}$

Let's substitute the information from the raisins Jackson bought into our equation. He bought $\greenD{5}$ ounces, and the total price was $\maroonD{\$4}$.

$\maroonD{4} = \greenD{5} \cdot \blueD{\text{price per ounce}}$

The raisins cost $\$0.80$ per ounce.

We can buy $1.25$ ounces of raisins for $\$1$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Amelia used $6$ liters of gasoline to drive $48$ kilometers.

**How many kilometers did Amelia drive per liter?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] kilometers

**At that rate, how many liters does it take to drive $1$ kilometer?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] liters
hints:
We can write a verbal equation to relate the amount of gasoline and the distance driven.

$\maroonD{\text{total distance}} = \greenD{\text{number of liters}} \cdot \blueD{\text{distance per liter}}$

Let's substitute the information from Amelia driving into our equation. She used $\greenD{6}$ liters, and the total distance was $\maroonD{48}$ kilometers.

$\maroonD{48} = \greenD{6} \cdot \blueD{\text{distance per liter}}$

Amelia drove $8$ kilometers per liter.

At that rate, it takes $0.125$ liters of gasoline to drive $1$ kilometer.

assessment_items.question_content:
Carys calculates the total amount $E$, in dollars, that she earns for working $h$ hours using the equation $E=10h$.

**How many dollars does Carys earn per hour?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars

**At that rate, how many hours does it take Carys to earn one dollar?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] hours
hints: First we want to know how much Carys earns for working $1$ hour. Since $h$ gives us the number of hours Carys works, we can substitute $h=1$ into the equation and solve for $E$.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{E}&=\greenD{10}\blueD{h}\\\\
\maroonD{E}&=\greenD{10}\cdot \blueD{1}\\\\
\maroonD{E}&=10
\end{align}$

Carys earns $10$ dollars per hour.

For the second question, we know the total amount Carys earns equals $\maroonD{1}$ dollar, but we do not know the number of hours. We'll substitute into our original equation again.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{E}&=\greenD{10}\blueD{h}\\\\
\maroonD{1}&=\greenD{10}\blueD{h}\\\\
\dfrac{\maroonD{1}}{10}&=\dfrac{\cancel{\greenD{10}}\blueD{h}}{\cancel{10}}\\\\
0.1&=\blueD{h}
\end{align}$

It takes Carys $0.1$ hours to earn $1$ dollar.

Carys earns $10$ dollars per hour.

It takes Carys $0.1$ hours to earn one dollar.

assessment_items.question_content:
We can calculate the depth $d$ of snow, in centimeters, that accumulates in Harper's yard during the first $h$ hours of a snowstorm using the equation $d=5h$.

**How many hours does it take for $1$ centimeter of snow to accumulate in Harper's yard?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] hours

**How many centimeters of snow accumulate per hour?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] centimeters
hints: First we want to know how many hours it takes for $1$ centimeter of snow to accumulate in Harper's yard. Since $d$ gives us the depth of the snow, we can substitute $d=1$ into the equation and solve for $h$.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{d}&=\greenD{5}\blueD{h}\\\\
\maroonD{1}&=\greenD{{5}}\blueD{h}\\\\
\dfrac{1}{5}\cdot\maroonD{1}&=\dfrac{1}{5}\cdot\greenD{{5}}\blueD{h}\\\\
\dfrac{1}{5}&=\blueD{h}
\end{align}$

It takes $\dfrac{1}{5}$ hour for $1$ centimeter of snow to accumulate in Harper's yard.

For the second question, we know the time elapsed equals $\blueD{1}$ hour, but we do not know the depth of the snow. We'll substitute into our original equation again.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{d}&=\greenD{{5}}\blueD{h}\\\\
\maroonD{d}&=\greenD{5}\cdot\blueD{1}\\\\
\maroonD{d}&=5
\end{align}$

In Harper's yard, $5$ centimeters of snow accumulate per hour.

It takes $\dfrac{1}{5}$ hour for $1$ centimeter of snow to accumulate in Harper's yard.

During the snowstorm, $5$ centimeters of snow accumulate per hour.

assessment_items.question_content:
The grass in Jamie's yard grew $16$ centimeters in $10$ days. It was growing at a constant rate.

**How many days did it take the grass to grow $1$ centimeter?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] days

**How many centimeters did the grass grow in $1$ day?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] centimeters
hints:
We can write a verbal equation to relate the number of days to the amount of growth.

$\maroonD{\text{total growth}} = \greenD{\text{number of days}} \cdot \blueD{\text{growth per day}}$

Let's substitute the information from Jamie's grass into our equation. The grass grew for $\greenD{10}$ days, and the total growth was $\maroonD{16}$ centimeters.

$\maroonD{16} = \greenD{10} \cdot \blueD{\text{growth per day}}$

It takes $0.625$ days for the grass to grow $1$ centimeter.

The grass grows $1.6$ centimeters in $1$ day.

assessment_items.question_content:
Bonnie is making a dipping sauce. She mixes $150$ milliliters of soy sauce with $100$ milliliters of vinegar.

**How much soy sauce does Bonnie mix with every $1$ milliliter of vinegar?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] milliliters

**How much vinegar does Bonnie mix with every $1$ milliliter of soy sauce?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] milliliters
hints:
We can write a verbal equation to relate the amount of soy sauce to the amount of vinegar.

$\maroonD{\text{amount of soy sauce}} = \greenD{\text{amount of vinegar}} \cdot \blueD{\text{soy sauce-to-vinegar ratio}}$

Let's substitute the information from Bonnie's dipping sauce into our equation. $\maroonD{150}$ milliliters of soy sauce is mixed with $\greenD{100}$ milliliters of vinegar.

$\maroonD{150} = \greenD{100} \cdot \blueD{\text{soy sauce-to-vinegar ratio}}$

Bonnie mixes $1.5$ milliliters of soy sauce with every $1$ milliliter of vinegar.

Bonnie mixes $\dfrac{2}{3}$ milliliter of vinegar with every $1$ milliliter of soy sauce.

assessment_items.question_content:
We can calculate $E$, the amount of euros that has the same value as $D$ U.S. dollars, using the equation $E=\dfrac{17}{20}D$.

**How many euros have the same value as $1$ U.S. dollar?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] euros

**How many U.S. dollars have the same value as $1$ euro?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] dollars
hints:
First we want to know how many euros have the same value as $1$ U.S. dollar. Since $D$ represents the amount of U.S. dollars, we can substitute $D=1$ into the equation and solve for $E$.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{E}&=\greenD{\dfrac{17}{20}}\blueD{D}\\\\
\maroonD{E}&=\greenD{\dfrac{17}{20}}\cdot\blueD{1}\\\\
\maroonD{E}&=\dfrac{17}{20} \\\\
\maroonD{E}&=0.85
\end{align}$

$0.85$ euros have the same value as $1$ U.S. dollar.

For the second question, we want to know how many U.S. dollars have the same value as $1$ euro. We can substitute into the original equation again.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{E}&=\greenD{\dfrac{17}{20}}\blueD{D}\\\\
\maroonD{1}&=\greenD{\dfrac{17}{20}}\blueD{D}\\\\
\dfrac{20}{17}\cdot\maroonD{1}&=\dfrac{20}{17}\cdot\greenD{\dfrac{17}{20}}\blueD{D}\\\\
\dfrac{20}{17}&=\blueD{D} 
\end{align}$

$\dfrac{20}{17}$ U.S. dollars have the same value as $1$ euro.

$0.85$ euro has the same value as $1$ U.S. dollar.

$\dfrac{20}{17}$ U.S. dollars have the same value as $1$ euro.
